docs.title: mykube
docs.text:
Access a remote Kubernetes cluster from anywhere — no VPN, no public IP required.

mykube tunnels kubectl traffic through a WebSocket relay with end-to-end encryption. The server runs on the cluster side, the client runs on your laptop, and a simple pairing code connects them.

  Laptop                        Relay (public)                     Cluster
 +----------+    WSS tunnel    +--------------+    WSS tunnel    +--------------+
 | kubectl   |--> 127.0.0.1 --| mykube-relay |--------------------| mykube server |--> kube-apiserver
 +----------+   (local proxy)  +--------------+                  +--------------+

docs.title: Why mykube?
docs.text:
• Zero infrastructure — no VPN servers, no bastion hosts, no firewall rules
  • End-to-end encrypted — X25519 + HKDF + AES-256-GCM; the relay never sees your credentials
  • MITM protection — pairing code is cryptographically bound into key derivation with SAS verification
  • Just works — single binary, one command on each side, kubectl works as usual
  • Corporate-friendly — full HTTP/HTTPS proxy support including TLS-intercepting proxies

docs.title: Server side (on a machine with cluster access)
docs.text:
mykube server

A pairing code is displayed (e.g. 71124175).

docs.title: Client side (your laptop)
docs.text:
mykube client
# Enter pairing code: 71124175

A subshell opens with KUBECONFIG set. Use kubectl as usual:

[mykube:my-cluster] user@host:~$ kubectl get nodes

Type exit to disconnect.

Get started How it works

docs.title: Global flags
docs.text:
These flags apply to both server and client commands.

Flag Default Description --relay-url https://mykube.onrender.com Relay server URL --proxy-ca — Path to PEM CA cert for TLS-intercepting proxies

docs.title: mykube server
docs.text:
Start the server side on a machine with cluster access. Reads the local kubeconfig, creates a session on the relay, and waits for a client to pair.

mykube server [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Default Description --kubeconfig $KUBECONFIG or ~/.kube/config Path to kubeconfig file --relay-url https://mykube.onrender.com Relay server URL --proxy-ca — Path to PEM CA cert for TLS-intercepting proxies

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Use default kubeconfig and public relay
mykube server

# Use a specific kubeconfig
mykube server --kubeconfig /path/to/kubeconfig

# Use a custom relay
mykube server --relay-url https://relay.example.com

docs.title: mykube client
docs.text:
Connect to a server via pairing code. After key exchange and verification, spawns a subshell with kubectl configured.

mykube client [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Default Description --relay-url https://mykube.onrender.com Relay server URL --proxy-ca — Path to PEM CA cert for TLS-intercepting proxies --no-shell false Don't spawn a subshell; print KUBECONFIG path and block until Ctrl+C

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Connect using the public relay
mykube client

# Use a custom relay
mykube client --relay-url https://relay.example.com

# Behind a corporate TLS-intercepting proxy
mykube client --proxy-ca /path/to/proxy-ca.pem

# Headless mode (no subshell)
mykube client --no-shell

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
cli/                          Go CLI (Cobra)
  cmd/                        Commands (root, server, client)
  internal/
    e2e/                      X25519 + HKDF + AES-256-GCM + SAS verification
    handshake/                Cluster metadata exchange
    kubeconfig/               Kubeconfig loading and writing
    relay/                    Relay HTTP/WS client
    tunnel/                   TCP <-> WebSocket multiplexing

relay/                        Python relay server (FastAPI)
  src/mykube_relay/
    routes/                   HTTP API + WebSocket handlers
    session_store.py          In-memory session management
    rate_limit.py             Per-IP rate limiting

docs.title: CLI (Go)
docs.text:
Requires Go 1.25+.

cd cli
go build ./...

Build with a version tag:

go build -ldflags="-s -w -X github.com/damien/mykube/cli/cmd.Version=v0.7.0" -o mykube

For static binaries, set CGO_ENABLED=0:

CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o mykube

docs.title: Relay (Python)
docs.text:
Requires Python 3.11+ and uv.

cd relay
uv sync

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
cd cli
go test ./...

docs.title: Relay
docs.text:
cd relay
uv run pytest

Tests use pytest-asyncio with asyncio_mode = "auto".

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
cd cli
go vet ./...

docs.title: Relay
docs.text:
cd relay
uv run ruff check .

docs.title: Relay
docs.text:
cd relay
uv run python -m mykube_relay

Starts on http://0.0.0.0:8000.

docs.title: CLI (against local relay)
docs.text:
# Terminal 1: server
cd cli
go run . server --relay-url http://localhost:8000

# Terminal 2: client
cd cli
go run . client --relay-url http://localhost:8000

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
Download a prebuilt binary from GitHub Releases, or use one of the methods below.

Linux (amd64)Linux (arm64)macOS (Apple Silicon)macOS (Intel)Windows 
curl -Lo mykube https://github.com/damhau/mykube/releases/latest/download/mykube-linux-amd64
chmod +x mykube
sudo mv mykube /usr/local/bin/

curl -Lo mykube https://github.com/damhau/mykube/releases/latest/download/mykube-linux-arm64
chmod +x mykube
sudo mv mykube /usr/local/bin/

curl -Lo mykube https://github.com/damhau/mykube/releases/latest/download/mykube-darwin-arm64
chmod +x mykube
sudo mv mykube /usr/local/bin/

curl -Lo mykube https://github.com/damhau/mykube/releases/latest/download/mykube-darwin-amd64
chmod +x mykube
sudo mv mykube /usr/local/bin/

Download mykube-windows-amd64.exe from the releases page and add it to your PATH.

docs.title: 1. Start the server
docs.text:
On a machine with access to your Kubernetes cluster:

mykube server

This reads your current kubeconfig, creates a session on the relay, and displays a pairing code:

Pairing code: 71124175
Waiting for client to connect...

docs.title: 2. Connect with the client
docs.text:
On your laptop:

mykube client

Enter the pairing code when prompted. After key exchange and SAS verification, a subshell opens:

Enter pairing code: 71124175
Verifying secure channel... OK
Connected to cluster "my-cluster"

[mykube:my-cluster] user@host:~$

docs.title: 3. Use kubectl
docs.text:
Inside the subshell, kubectl is configured to tunnel through mykube:

kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl logs deployment/my-app
kubectl exec -it pod/my-app -- /bin/sh

All commands work as if you were on the cluster network.

docs.title: 4. Disconnect
docs.text: Type exit or press Ctrl+D to leave the subshell. The temporary kubeconfig is deleted and the tunnel is closed.

docs.title: Headless mode
docs.text:
If you don't want a subshell (e.g. for scripts or CI), use --no-shell:

mykube client --no-shell

This prints the KUBECONFIG path and blocks until Ctrl+C. You can use it in another terminal:

export KUBECONFIG=/tmp/mykube-my-cluster-abc123.yaml
kubectl get nodes

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
mykube has three components:

  • Server (cluster side) — a Go binary that reads the local kubeconfig and tunnels kube-apiserver access through the relay
  • Client (laptop side) — a Go binary that pairs with the server, receives credentials, and provides a local kubectl endpoint
  • Relay (public) — a Python FastAPI app that brokers WebSocket connections between server and client
  Laptop                        Relay (public)                     Cluster
 +----------+    WSS tunnel    +--------------+    WSS tunnel    +--------------+
 | kubectl   |--> 127.0.0.1 --| mykube-relay |--------------------| mykube server |--> kube-apiserver
 +----------+   (local proxy)  +--------------+                  +--------------+

The relay is untrusted — it only forwards opaque encrypted blobs between the two sides.

docs.title: 1. Session creation
docs.text: The server calls POST /api/sessions on the relay. The relay returns a session_id and an 8-digit pairing code.

docs.title: 2. Pairing
docs.text: The client calls POST /api/pair with the pairing code. The relay associates the client with the session.

docs.title: 3. WebSocket connection
docs.text:
Both sides open WebSocket connections to the relay:

  • Server: /ws/agent/{session_id}
  • Client: /ws/client/{session_id}

The relay forwards messages between the two WebSockets.

docs.title: 4. Key exchange
docs.text:
Both sides perform an X25519 ECDH key exchange:

  1. Each side generates an ephemeral X25519 key pair
  2. Public keys are exchanged over the WebSocket
  3. A shared secret is derived via X25519
  4. An AES-256-GCM session key is derived using HKDF-SHA256 with the pairing code as salt

Binding the pairing code into key derivation means an attacker who intercepts the key exchange but doesn't know the code will derive a different key.

docs.title: 5. SAS verification
docs.text:
After key derivation, both sides compute a Short Authentication String (a verification tag) from the shared secret and pairing code. They exchange these tags over the encrypted channel. If the tags don't match, it means a man-in-the-middle has substituted public keys, and the connection is aborted.

This approach is inspired by Magic Wormhole.

docs.title: 6. Credential transfer
docs.text: The server sends the cluster metadata (name, CA certificate, credentials) encrypted over the tunnel. The client decrypts it and writes a temporary kubeconfig.

docs.title: 7. TCP multiplexing
docs.text:
The client starts a local TCP listener (on 127.0.0.1). When kubectl connects, the connection is multiplexed over the single encrypted WebSocket tunnel.

Binary framing: each WebSocket binary frame contains a 4-byte connection ID followed by the payload. Text frames are used for control messages (new:{id} to open a connection, done:{id} to close it).

This allows multiple concurrent kubectl operations (e.g. kubectl exec, kubectl port-forward, parallel API calls) over a single WebSocket.

docs.title: Proxy Support
docs.text: mykube fully supports HTTP/HTTPS proxies, including corporate TLS-intercepting proxies.

docs.title: Standard proxy variables
docs.text:
mykube respects the standard environment variables:

Variable Description HTTP_PROXY Proxy URL for HTTP connections HTTPS_PROXY Proxy URL for HTTPS connections NO_PROXY Comma-separated list of hosts to bypass the proxy

All HTTP and WebSocket traffic to the relay will be routed through the proxy automatically.

export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.example.com:8080
mykube client

docs.title: TLS-intercepting proxies
docs.text:
Corporate environments often use TLS-intercepting proxies (e.g. Squid, Zscaler, BlueCoat) that decrypt and re-encrypt HTTPS traffic. These proxies present their own CA certificate, which mykube won't trust by default.

Use --proxy-ca to provide the proxy's CA certificate:

mykube client --proxy-ca /path/to/proxy-ca.pem
mykube server --proxy-ca /path/to/proxy-ca.pem

The CA file should be a PEM-encoded certificate. This flag applies to both the HTTP API calls and the WebSocket connection to the relay.

Note

Even with a TLS-intercepting proxy, your kubectl traffic remains end-to-end encrypted between client and server. The proxy can only see the outer WebSocket connection to the relay, not the encrypted tunnel contents.

docs.title: Finding your proxy CA
docs.text:
ZscalerCorporate ITSquid 
# Usually available at:
/usr/local/share/ca-certificates/zscaler-root-ca.pem
# Or export from your browser's certificate store

Ask your IT department for the proxy CA certificate in PEM format. It's often distributed via system policy or available on an internal portal.

The CA certificate is configured in squid.conf under ssl_bump. The admin can provide the public CA cert.

docs.title: Encryption
docs.text:
All traffic between the client and server is end-to-end encrypted. The relay only sees opaque binary blobs.

Layer Algorithm Key exchange X25519 ECDH Key derivation HKDF-SHA256 (pairing code as salt) Encryption AES-256-GCM

docs.title: MITM protection
docs.text:
The pairing code is cryptographically bound into key derivation via the HKDF salt. After key exchange, both sides derive and exchange a SAS (Short Authentication String) verification tag over the encrypted channel. If a man-in-the-middle substitutes public keys, the derived keys will differ and the SAS tags won't match — the connection is aborted.

This is inspired by Magic Wormhole's approach of binding a short code into the cryptographic handshake.

docs.title: Pairing codes
docs.text:
• 8-digit numeric codes generated with Python's secrets module (cryptographic PRNG)
  • Maximum 5 attempts per session — after 5 wrong codes, the session is destroyed
  • Sessions expire after 120 seconds if unpaired

docs.title: Relay trust model
docs.text:
The relay is untrusted by design:

  • It never sees decrypted traffic
  • It cannot forge or inject frames (all data is authenticated by AES-256-GCM)
  • It only forwards WebSocket messages between paired sessions
  • A single agent and single client connection is enforced per session

You can use the public relay at mykube.onrender.com or self-host your own.

docs.title: Additional protections
docs.text: Protection Details Rate limiting 20 requests/min per IP on API endpoints Session hijacking Duplicate agent/client connections are rejected Temp file permissions Kubeconfig written with mode 0600 to a random path, deleted on exit Input sanitization Cluster names stripped to [a-zA-Z0-9._-] before use in paths and shell commands WebSocket message size 16 MiB limit on both CLI and relay

docs.title: Known issues tracker
docs.text: The table below tracks all identified security issues and their status.

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: # Issue Fix 1 K8s credentials exposed to relay E2E encryption (X25519 + HKDF + AES-256-GCM + SAS) 4 Weak pairing code / non-crypto PRNG secrets module, 8-digit codes 5 World-readable temp file os.CreateTemp (mode 0600, random path) 6 Path traversal via ClusterName SanitizeClusterName strips to [a-zA-Z0-9._-] 7 Shell injection via ClusterName Same sanitization applied in RC file 8 YAML injection via ClusterName Sanitized name used in template 9 No rate limiting 20 req/min per IP on /api/* 11 No WS message size limit 16 MiB limit on CLI and relay 13 Session hijacking (duplicate connections) Reject if agent/client already connected 14 ConnID spoofing E2E encryption means relay can't forge frames 15 Unbounded WS reads SetReadLimit(16 MiB) on both sides 18 Symlink attack on temp file os.CreateTemp with O_EXCL 20 random instead of secrets Uses secrets.choice

docs.title: Open
docs.text: # Issue Severity 2 insecure-skip-tls-verify in generated kubeconfig Critical 3 No authentication on relay endpoints Critical 10 No WebSocket Origin validation High 12 Unbounded session count Medium 16 No handshake field validation Medium 17 No CORS middleware Medium 19 Busy-wait polling loops Low 21 Broad except Exception: pass Low

docs.title: Relay Overview
docs.text: The relay is a lightweight Python service that brokers WebSocket connections between mykube servers and clients. It is untrusted by design — it never sees decrypted traffic.

docs.title: What the relay does
docs.text:
1. Session management — creates sessions with pairing codes, matches servers to clients
  2. WebSocket forwarding — forwards binary and text frames between paired server/client connections
  3. Rate limiting — throttles API requests to prevent abuse (20 req/min per IP)
  4. Connection enforcement — ensures only one server and one client per session

docs.title: What the relay does NOT do
docs.text:
• Decrypt or inspect traffic (all data is encrypted end-to-end)
  • Store credentials or sensitive data
  • Authenticate users (sessions are matched by pairing codes)
  • Persist any data (everything is in-memory)

docs.title: Trust model
docs.text:
The relay is designed to be completely untrusted:

  • Confidentiality: All traffic is encrypted with AES-256-GCM. The relay can't read it.
  • Integrity: Frames are authenticated. The relay can't modify or forge data.
  • Availability: The relay can drop or delay messages, but it can't compromise security.

The worst a malicious relay can do is refuse to forward messages (denial of service).

docs.title: Public relay
docs.text:
A free public relay is available at mykube.onrender.com and is used by default. No setup required.

For organizations that prefer to control the relay infrastructure, see Self-Hosting.

docs.title: API endpoints
docs.text: Method Path Description POST /api/sessions Create a new session (returns session ID + pairing code) POST /api/pair Pair a client with a session using the pairing code GET /ws/agent/{id} WebSocket endpoint for the server (agent) side GET /ws/client/{id} WebSocket endpoint for the client side

docs.title: Self-Hosting the Relay
docs.text: The relay is a lightweight FastAPI application that you can run anywhere.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • uv (recommended) or pip

docs.title: Running locally
docs.text:
cd relay
uv run python -m mykube_relay

The relay starts on http://0.0.0.0:8000 by default.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
All configuration is via environment variables:

Variable Default Description SESSION_TTL_WAITING 120 Seconds before an unpaired session expires SESSION_TTL_PAIRED 3600 Seconds before a paired session expires MAX_PAIR_ATTEMPTS 5 Max wrong pairing attempts before session is destroyed HOST 0.0.0.0 Listen address PORT 8000 Listen port

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app
COPY relay/ .

RUN pip install uv && uv sync

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uv", "run", "python", "-m", "mykube_relay"]

docker build -t mykube-relay .
docker run -p 8000:8000 mykube-relay

docs.title: Using your relay
docs.text:
Point both server and client to your relay URL:

mykube server --relay-url https://relay.example.com
mykube client --relay-url https://relay.example.com

docs.title: Deployment considerations
docs.text:
• TLS: Put the relay behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, etc.) with TLS. WebSocket connections require wss:// in production.
  • Memory: Sessions are stored in-memory. The relay uses minimal resources but will lose all sessions on restart.
  • Scaling: The relay is single-instance by design (in-memory session store). For high availability, consider sticky sessions or a shared session backend.
  • Monitoring: The relay logs session creation, pairing, and WebSocket connections to stdout.
